CONFIGURED MODE (T173), ROUND 2: You are ONE OF SEVERAL plan reviewers for plan-flow goal **G1** (project: fecalia, ledger root = /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia). RETURN your verdict as a single fenced ```json block; write NOTHING to the reviews ledger.

Round 1 returned `revise` (review R1). The plan has been revised to address all three reconciled criticisms. VERIFY the fixes and re-judge the whole plan.

The three round-1 criticisms and the applied fixes:
1. Pacing/bufferbloat (a KEY RISK) was unowned → T9 now lists congestion/bufferbloat + send-pacing as a 7th P0-findings area (with a measurement + verdict on whether the scheduler must pace); T21 (weighted aggregation) now includes send-pacing/bufferbloat control with a pacing acceptance clause.
2. Runtime path add/remove was missing → NEW task T30 (milestone M5/P1, dependsOn T12,T13) covers adding/removing a path at runtime without disturbing existing paths or the WG session.
3. Spurious edge T21→T17 → removed; T21 now dependsOn [T15,T18]; T23 (P2 e2e) now dependsOn [T21,T17] so the /metrics consumer declares the edge directly. (This was YOUR round-1 finding — confirm it is correctly applied.)

Read: fetch_item("goals","G1"); fetch_item("reviews","R1") for the round-1 criticisms; Read /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/fec-prompt.md; the answered questions (fts_search ledger:questions milestone:M1); and the current task set — fetch_ledger("tasks") and inspect T1..T30, especially the changed/added T9, T21, T23, T30. Confirm the DAG is still acyclic and the three fixes are genuinely and correctly applied, and do a fresh completeness/sequencing/testability pass for anything else material.

Return EXACTLY:
```json
{ "summary": "<one-line verdict>", "verdict": "go-ahead" | "revise", "new_questions": [], "criticism": ["<remaining actionable defect, if any>"], "defects": [] }
```
`verdict` MUST be exactly `go-ahead` or `revise`; a `revise` MUST carry >=1 criticism or new_question. Give `go-ahead` if the fixes are correctly applied and no NEW material defect remains — do not invent marginal nits to justify another round. End with a `### Session summary`.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

{"item":{"id":"G1","milestoneId":"M1","status":"planning","fields":{"title":"wanbond: 2-WAN bonding tunnel with adaptive FEC on amneziawg-go + custom conn.Bind — implementation and test harness","description":"Plan the implementation and test harness for `wanbond`, per the full project prompt in `fec-prompt.md` (repo root — authoritative source; summarized here).

GOAL: Build `wanbond` — a single self-contained Go binary (runs on both edge and concentrator) that bonds two unreliable, heterogeneous WANs (Starlink: low-latency, jittery, intermittent obstruction loss; 4G/5G: metered, stable, variable latency) into one resilient, DPI-resistant tunnel for GENERAL IP traffic, with adaptive FEC.

ARCHITECTURE (decided — do NOT re-evaluate): embed amneziawg-go as a library (unmodified WG engine: TUN, Noise, AEAD, rekey, roaming, keepalive); ALL bonding logic (multipath scheduling, RS-FEC, resequencing, per-path telemetry) in a custom `conn.Bind` under the engine, operating on opaque encrypted WG datagrams. Outer bonding frame (outer-seq, path-id, fec-group, flags) + parity/probe/control frame types. Inner layer fail-closed; outer control/probe frames PSK-authenticated; outer data headers unauthenticated (DoS-grade risk accepted). Outer wire format = unidentifiable high-entropy UDP (no magic bytes).

PRIORITIZED REQUIREMENTS (earlier must not regress for later): 1. transparent failover (TCP flow survives WAN death, no reset); 2. data-thrift (metered 5G ~idle until needed); 3. bandwidth aggregation on demand; 4. RS-FEC masking loss without duplication; 5. adaptive FEC tracking measured per-path loss; 6. DPI resistance (no WG fingerprint; amnezia junk params as defense-in-depth; protocol mimicry out of scope).

PHASES: P0 spike (embed amneziawg-go + trivial pass-through Bind, document conn.Bind contract pitfalls, baseline throughput) → P1 transparent failover MVP → P2 aggregation + data-thrift → P3 fixed-ratio FEC → P4 adaptive FEC → P5 DPI hardening (capture + entropy check; nDPI/Suricata must not classify as WireGuard). Each phase independently shippable + verifiable.

TEST HARNESS is an explicit first-class deliverable: per-phase verification criteria are in the prompt (kill-active-WAN-mid-SSH survival; bonded throughput ≈ sum; ≥Y% recovery at X% injected loss with ≤Z% overhead; adaptive overhead ≤ fixed-FEC baseline; DPI classification checks). Expect network emulation (loss/jitter/reorder injection, e.g. netns + netem) to make these reproducible.

KEY RISKS (investigate first, P0 targets most): conn.Bind API impedance (batched send/recv, GSO/GRO, Endpoint identity when hiding N paths behind one virtual endpoint); amneziawg-go fork lag vs upstream (keep Bind portable to wireguard-go); WG anti-replay window vs multipath reorder (own outer-seq, never reuse inner counter); reorder-buffer tuning vs Starlink jitter; adaptive control-loop STABILITY (the crux); FEC grouping latency vs recovery; bufferbloat/pacing; hotels blocking UDP wholesale (documented limitation, no TCP fallback).

CONTEXT: edge = Linux box behind router that already pins source IPs to WANs (path selection external); concentrator = small public-IP VPS that NATs tunnel traffic; Starlink ~45ms jittery, 5G ~64ms stable; start with 2 paths, design for N.

NON-GOALS: not a general SD-WAN product; no GUI; not >3 links initially; path pinning external; no TCP/TLS fallback transport; no protocol mimicry; base-library decision closed (kcp-go, quic-go, plain wireguard-go rejected in favor of amneziawg-go + custom Bind).

LIBRARIES: amneziawg-go (base, decided); klauspost/reedsolomon (RS FEC).","sessionLogs":[".cq/logs/20260701-231505-aacec84bd6a7748f4.md",".cq/logs/20260701-234215-a533f3a14c0afe112.md",".cq/logs/20260701-234215-a2ee01f9272ece9de.md"],"rawLogs":[".cq/logs/raw/20260701-231505-aacec84bd6a7748f4.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260701-234215-a533f3a14c0afe112.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260701-234215-a2ee01f9272ece9de.jsonl"],"milestones":["M2","M3","M4","M5","M6","M7","M8","M9"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-01T23:11:54.649Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-01T23:42:24.947Z","author":"fable-5","session":"0047802a-1b44-4fcc-8198-d12359610ad6"}}

1	# wanbond — cq project prompt
2	
3	Paste into `/cq:plan`.
4	
5	---
6	
7	GOAL: Build `wanbond` — a single self-contained Go binary (runs on both edge and
8	concentrator) that bonds two unreliable, heterogeneous WANs (Starlink: low-latency,
9	jittery, intermittent obstruction loss; 4G/5G: metered, stable, variable latency)
10	into one resilient, DPI-resistant tunnel for GENERAL IP traffic, with adaptive FEC.
11	No FOSS tool combines transparent failover + aggregation + data-efficient adaptive
12	FEC; this fills that gap, tuned for the 2-link case.
13	
14	ARCHITECTURE (decided — do NOT re-evaluate the base choice):
15	- Embed amneziawg-go (github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go, WireGuard fork with DPI
16	  obfuscation, same Noise crypto) as a LIBRARY: its device engine provides TUN I/O,
17	  Noise handshake, AEAD, rekeying, peer roaming, persistent keepalive — all UNMODIFIED.
18	- All bonding logic lives in a custom `conn.Bind` transport implementation UNDER the
19	  WG engine (precedent: Tailscale magicsock does transparent path migration this way).
20	  The Bind owns one UDP socket per path (bound to its source address) and operates on
21	  OPAQUE encrypted WG datagrams — it needs no crypto of its own for the data plane.
22	- Outer bonding frame wraps each WG datagram: (outer-seq, path-id, fec-group, flags).
23	  Additional outer frame types: RS parity, per-path probes, path control.
24	- Receive side: unwrap -> FEC recovery -> resequencing buffer -> deliver to the WG
25	  engine as if from a single socket. WG sees ONE stable virtual endpoint per peer;
26	  the Bind privately manages the real per-path endpoints and their roaming.
27	- Security model: inner WG layer is fail-closed (injection/decryption impossible
28	  without keys). Outer DATA headers are unauthenticated — forgery costs only bounded
29	  buffer/decode work (DoS-grade, acceptable). Outer CONTROL/PROBE frames are
30	  authenticated via a static pre-shared key from config (HMAC or AEAD — vetted lib,
31	  not hand-rolled). Outer wire format must carry no plaintext magic constants and
32	  look like high-entropy UDP (see requirement 6).
33	
34	CONTEXT / GIVEN:
35	- Edge = Linux box behind a router that ALREADY pins each path to a WAN (traffic from
36	  source IP A egresses Starlink, from source IP B egresses 5G). Path-to-WAN selection
37	  is SOLVED externally; the tunnel only binds one UDP socket per path source.
38	- Concentrator = small low-latency VPS, public IP, NATs tunnel traffic to the internet.
39	- Measured links: Starlink ~45ms (jittery), 5G ~64ms (stable). Start with 2 paths,
40	  design for N.
41	- Edge is sometimes used from hostile networks (hotels etc.) that block or throttle
42	  identified VPN protocols.
43	
44	PRIORITIZED REQUIREMENTS (in order — earlier ones must not regress for later ones):
45	1. TRANSPARENT FAILOVER: a TCP flow survives a WAN dying with NO connection reset.
46	2. DATA-THRIFT: never duplicate all traffic; keep metered 5G ~idle until needed.
47	3. BANDWIDTH AGGREGATION on demand (single flow can use both links).
48	4. FEC: Reed-Solomon parity that masks loss without full duplication.
49	5. ADAPTIVE FEC: redundancy scales with measured per-path loss (low when clean).
50	6. DPI RESISTANCE: on-wire traffic must not fingerprint as WireGuard. The custom
51	   outer framing already destroys the WG wire signature; keep it free of magic
52	   bytes / fixed offsets and expose amnezia junk/obfuscation params (Jc/Jmin/Jmax,
53	   S1/S2, H1-H4) as defense-in-depth. NOTE: amnezia 2.0 protocol MIMICRY
54	   (QUIC/DNS/SIP lookalike) is destroyed by our outer wrapping — treat it as out of
55	   scope; target is "unidentifiable high-entropy UDP", not protocol mimicry.
56	
57	FUNCTIONAL:
58	- TUN I/O, handshake, encryption, rekey: provided by the embedded amneziawg-go engine.
59	- Custom conn.Bind: per-path UDP socket bound to a source address; endpoint roaming
60	  per path; outer framing as above; MTU accounting for outer header + WG overhead
61	  (no fragmentation / ICMP black holes; MSS clamping guidance).

63	  applied BEFORE delivery to WG (its anti-replay window then never sees pathological

65	- Per-path quality measurement (RTT/loss/jitter) via PSK-authenticated probe frames
66	  + outer-seq gap accounting. WG keepalive is per-peer, NOT per-path — path liveness
67	  is entirely ours.
68	- Scheduler: active-backup (data-thrift) -> weighted aggregation under load, FEC-aware.
69	- RS FEC over outer frames: group + emit parity within a deadline; receiver recovers
70	  <=K losses/group. FEC operates on ciphertext — content-agnostic.
71	- Adaptive control loop: adjust FEC ratio + scheduler weights with hysteresis (no thrash).
72	- Path up/down + add/remove; survive edge public-IP change on any path (mobile).
73

75	- Robust while mobile (path flaps, IP changes) — must never wedge; fail-closed security
76	  (carries 100% of traffic + holds keys); single static binary + systemd + simple config
77	  (WG keys/peers + amnezia params + path list + PSK); metrics/logging per path
78	  (loss/RTT/throughput/FEC-overhead).

80	  (API drift hedge: the fork lags upstream; swapping bases must stay cheap).
81

83	- P0 (SPIKE, timeboxed) = embed amneziawg-go with a trivial single-socket pass-through
84	  Bind. Verify: tunnel passes traffic edge<->concentrator; measure baseline throughput;
85	  document conn.Bind contract pitfalls (batched send/recv semantics, GSO/GRO paths,
86	  Endpoint identity model, amnezia junk packets arriving at the Bind).
87	- P1 (MVP) = transparent failover (single active path + per-path roaming). Verify: kill
88	  the active WAN mid-SSH/download -> session survives, recovers within N seconds.
89	- P2 = aggregation + data-thrift policy. Verify: bonded throughput ~ sum; 5G bytes ~0 while
90	  Starlink healthy.
91	- P3 = fixed-ratio FEC. Verify: at injected X% loss, >=Y% recovered w/o retransmit; overhead <=Z%.
92	- P4 = adaptive FEC. Verify: overhead tracks loss; total data <= fixed-FEC baseline for same masking.
93	- P5 = DPI hardening pass. Verify: outer frames show no fixed bytes/offsets (capture +
94	  entropy check); amnezia params configurable end-to-end; nDPI/Suricata do not classify
95	  the flow as WireGuard.
96	
97	LIBRARIES (evaluate within the decided architecture):
98	- amneziawg-go (base, decided) | klauspost/reedsolomon (RS FEC, de-facto standard).
99	
100	KEY RISKS TO INVESTIGATE FIRST (P0 targets most of these):
101	- conn.Bind API impedance: batch-oriented ReceiveFunc/Send, GSO/GRO fast paths,
102	  Endpoint identity semantics when hiding N paths behind one virtual endpoint.
103	- amneziawg-go fork lag vs upstream wireguard-go (missing perf work / API drift).
104	- WG anti-replay window vs multipath reordering (expected fine at our pps + skew, but
105	  measure); rekey epochs — use OWN outer-seq, do not reuse the inner WG counter.
106	- Reorder-buffer tuning vs Starlink jitter; adaptive control-loop STABILITY (the crux);

109	  as a known limitation (TCP/TLS fallback transport is a non-goal for now).
110

112	out of scope (done externally); no TCP/TLS fallback transport; no protocol mimicry

file.content:
# wanbond — cq project prompt

Paste into `/cq:plan`.

---

GOAL: Build `wanbond` — a single self-contained Go binary (runs on both edge and
concentrator) that bonds two unreliable, heterogeneous WANs (Starlink: low-latency,
jittery, intermittent obstruction loss; 4G/5G: metered, stable, variable latency)
into one resilient, DPI-resistant tunnel for GENERAL IP traffic, with adaptive FEC.
No FOSS tool combines transparent failover + aggregation + data-efficient adaptive
FEC; this fills that gap, tuned for the 2-link case.

ARCHITECTURE (decided — do NOT re-evaluate the base choice):
- Embed amneziawg-go (github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go, WireGuard fork with DPI
  obfuscation, same Noise crypto) as a LIBRARY: its device engine provides TUN I/O,
  Noise handshake, AEAD, rekeying, peer roaming, persistent keepalive — all UNMODIFIED.
- All bonding logic lives in a custom `conn.Bind` transport implementation UNDER the
  WG engine (precedent: Tailscale magicsock does transparent path migration this way).
  The Bind owns one UDP socket per path (bound to its source address) and operates on
  OPAQUE encrypted WG datagrams — it needs no crypto of its own for the data plane.
- Outer bonding frame wraps each WG datagram: (outer-seq, path-id, fec-group, flags).
  Additional outer frame types: RS parity, per-path probes, path control.
- Receive side: unwrap -> FEC recovery -> resequencing buffer -> deliver to the WG
  engine as if from a single socket. WG sees ONE stable virtual endpoint per peer;
  the Bind privately manages the real per-path endpoints and their roaming.
- Security model: inner WG layer is fail-closed (injection/decryption impossible
  without keys). Outer DATA headers are unauthenticated — forgery costs only bounded
  buffer/decode work (DoS-grade, acceptable). Outer CONTROL/PROBE frames are
  authenticated via a static pre-shared key from config (HMAC or AEAD — vetted lib,
  not hand-rolled). Outer wire format must carry no plaintext magic constants and
  look like high-entropy UDP (see requirement 6).

CONTEXT / GIVEN:
- Edge = Linux box behind a router that ALREADY pins each path to a WAN (traffic from
  source IP A egresses Starlink, from source IP B egresses 5G). Path-to-WAN selection
  is SOLVED externally; the tunnel only binds one UDP socket per path source.
- Concentrator = small low-latency VPS, public IP, NATs tunnel traffic to the internet.
- Measured links: Starlink ~45ms (jittery), 5G ~64ms (stable). Start with 2 paths,
  design for N.
- Edge is sometimes used from hostile networks (hotels etc.) that block or throttle
  identified VPN protocols.

PRIORITIZED REQUIREMENTS (in order — earlier ones must not regress for later ones):
1. TRANSPARENT FAILOVER: a TCP flow survives a WAN dying with NO connection reset.
2. DATA-THRIFT: never duplicate all traffic; keep metered 5G ~idle until needed.
3. BANDWIDTH AGGREGATION on demand (single flow can use both links).
4. FEC: Reed-Solomon parity that masks loss without full duplication.
5. ADAPTIVE FEC: redundancy scales with measured per-path loss (low when clean).
6. DPI RESISTANCE: on-wire traffic must not fingerprint as WireGuard. The custom
   outer framing already destroys the WG wire signature; keep it free of magic
   bytes / fixed offsets and expose amnezia junk/obfuscation params (Jc/Jmin/Jmax,
   S1/S2, H1-H4) as defense-in-depth. NOTE: amnezia 2.0 protocol MIMICRY
   (QUIC/DNS/SIP lookalike) is destroyed by our outer wrapping — treat it as out of
   scope; target is "unidentifiable high-entropy UDP", not protocol mimicry.

FUNCTIONAL:
- TUN I/O, handshake, encryption, rekey: provided by the embedded amneziawg-go engine.
- Custom conn.Bind: per-path UDP socket bound to a source address; endpoint roaming
  per path; outer framing as above; MTU accounting for outer header + WG overhead
  (no fragmentation / ICMP black holes; MSS clamping guidance).
- Receive resequencing buffer (bounded window + timeout) for multipath reordering,
  applied BEFORE delivery to WG (its anti-replay window then never sees pathological
  reorder; verify window size gives margin anyway).
- Per-path quality measurement (RTT/loss/jitter) via PSK-authenticated probe frames
  + outer-seq gap accounting. WG keepalive is per-peer, NOT per-path — path liveness
  is entirely ours.
- Scheduler: active-backup (data-thrift) -> weighted aggregation under load, FEC-aware.
- RS FEC over outer frames: group + emit parity within a deadline; receiver recovers
  <=K losses/group. FEC operates on ciphertext — content-agnostic.
- Adaptive control loop: adjust FEC ratio + scheduler weights with hysteresis (no thrash).
- Path up/down + add/remove; survive edge public-IP change on any path (mobile).

NON-FUNCTIONAL:
- Robust while mobile (path flaps, IP changes) — must never wedge; fail-closed security
  (carries 100% of traffic + holds keys); single static binary + systemd + simple config
  (WG keys/peers + amnezia params + path list + PSK); metrics/logging per path
  (loss/RTT/throughput/FEC-overhead).
- Keep the Bind implementation portable across amneziawg-go and upstream wireguard-go
  (API drift hedge: the fork lags upstream; swapping bases must stay cheap).

PHASES (each independently shippable + verifiable):
- P0 (SPIKE, timeboxed) = embed amneziawg-go with a trivial single-socket pass-through
  Bind. Verify: tunnel passes traffic edge<->concentrator; measure baseline throughput;
  document conn.Bind contract pitfalls (batched send/recv semantics, GSO/GRO paths,
  Endpoint identity model, amnezia junk packets arriving at the Bind).
- P1 (MVP) = transparent failover (single active path + per-path roaming). Verify: kill
  the active WAN mid-SSH/download -> session survives, recovers within N seconds.
- P2 = aggregation + data-thrift policy. Verify: bonded throughput ~ sum; 5G bytes ~0 while
  Starlink healthy.
- P3 = fixed-ratio FEC. Verify: at injected X% loss, >=Y% recovered w/o retransmit; overhead <=Z%.
- P4 = adaptive FEC. Verify: overhead tracks loss; total data <= fixed-FEC baseline for same masking.
- P5 = DPI hardening pass. Verify: outer frames show no fixed bytes/offsets (capture +
  entropy check); amnezia params configurable end-to-end; nDPI/Suricata do not classify
  the flow as WireGuard.

LIBRARIES (evaluate within the decided architecture):
- amneziawg-go (base, decided) | klauspost/reedsolomon (RS FEC, de-facto standard).

KEY RISKS TO INVESTIGATE FIRST (P0 targets most of these):
- conn.Bind API impedance: batch-oriented ReceiveFunc/Send, GSO/GRO fast paths,
  Endpoint identity semantics when hiding N paths behind one virtual endpoint.
- amneziawg-go fork lag vs upstream wireguard-go (missing perf work / API drift).
- WG anti-replay window vs multipath reordering (expected fine at our pps + skew, but
  measure); rekey epochs — use OWN outer-seq, do not reuse the inner WG counter.
- Reorder-buffer tuning vs Starlink jitter; adaptive control-loop STABILITY (the crux);
  FEC grouping latency vs recovery; congestion/bufferbloat (need pacing?).
- Hostile networks that block UDP wholesale (hotels): no in-scope mitigation — document
  as a known limitation (TCP/TLS fallback transport is a non-goal for now).

NON-GOALS: not a general SD-WAN product; no GUI; not >3 links initially; path pinning is
out of scope (done externally); no TCP/TLS fallback transport; no protocol mimicry
(amnezia 2.0 style); do not re-evaluate the base-library decision (kcp-go, quic-go,
plain wireguard-go were considered and rejected in favor of amneziawg-go + custom Bind).

All reads complete: goal G1, review R1, fec-prompt.md, the eight answered questions (Q1–Q8), and the full task set T1–T30 across milestones M2–M9.

**Fix verification (the three round-1 criticisms):**

1. **Pacing/bufferbloat** — correctly applied. T9's description now enumerates seven P0 areas, with (7) "congestion/bufferbloat and whether send-pacing is needed" including a concrete measurement (standing queue / latency-under-load on emulated paths) and a verdict requirement; the acceptance requires findings for "each of the SEVEN named pitfall areas including the pacing/bufferbloat measurement". T21 now owns send-pacing/bufferbloat control conditioned on T9's verdict ("make pacing a no-op if T9 concludes it is unnecessary") with an operational acceptance clause (per-path egress bounded by the pace, no unbounded send backlog under sustained overload). The risk now has both an investigation owner and an implementation owner.

2. **Runtime path add/remove** — T30 exists (M5, `dependsOn [T12,T13]`), covers add (open socket, probe, admit when healthy) and remove (drain, close), with acceptance asserting zero TCP reset and no disturbance to the other path or the WG session. This closes the FUNCTIONAL "add/remove" + "design for N" gap. However, see the criticism below on its dependency edges.

3. **Spurious T21→T17 edge** — correctly applied (this was my round-1 finding). T21 `dependsOn` is now exactly `[T15,T18]` (telemetry reaches the scheduler via T13→T15, not via the HTTP export), and T23, the actual /metrics consumer, now declares `dependsOn [T21,T17]` directly. The edge semantics now reflect real prerequisites.

**DAG check:** all 30 tasks present; every `dependsOn` edge points to a strictly lower task id, so the graph is trivially acyclic. Phase gating (T10 checkpoint between P0 and P1) intact.

**Fresh pass — one new sequencing defect, introduced by the T30 fix itself:** T30's acceptance is scheduler-observable — "adding a second [path] at runtime brings it **into the scheduler** once its probes report healthy and **traffic begins using it**", and on removal "the flow **continues on the remaining path**" (i.e. failover). That behavior is implemented by T15 (active-backup scheduler v1, which also "expose[s] hooks the later ... scheduler extends"). T30 `dependsOn [T12,T13]` omits T15, so under the implement-flow's DAG-ready parallel dispatch, T30 becomes ready simultaneously with T15: it would be dispatched before/alongside the scheduler it must integrate with, cannot meet its acceptance, and both tasks would concurrently edit the scheduler package.
